title: BAS System Head-End Installation
description: A building automation system head-end has been set up in the IT/controls room. Review the installation for missing components.
presentItems: BAS server workstation with operator software installed
Network switch connecting BAS server to IP controllers
UPS battery backup for server
Monitor, keyboard, and mouse at operator station
BACnet/IP communication established to field controllers

missingItems.name: Automatic server data backup system
missingItems.explanation: BAS databases, graphics, programming, and trend data must be backed up automatically on a regular schedule. Without backup, a server failure results in complete loss of all building control configurations and historical data. Required by ASHRAE Guideline 13.

missingItems.name: BACnet Broadcast Management Device (BBMD)
missingItems.explanation: In multi-subnet BACnet/IP installations, a BBMD is required to forward broadcast messages between subnets. Without it, devices on different subnets cannot discover or communicate with each other. Required by ASHRAE 135 for routed IP networks.

explanation: BAS head-end installations require data backup to protect against catastrophic data loss and proper BACnet network infrastructure (BBMD) for multi-subnet communication. Both are frequently overlooked during initial commissioning.

title: VAV Box Controls Installation
description: A VAV terminal box with hot water reheat has been installed with its controls. Identify what's missing from the controls installation.
presentItems: DDC VAV controller mounted on box
Zone temperature sensor on wall in served space
Damper actuator connected to VAV damper
Hot water reheat valve with actuator
Controller wired to BAS network trunk

missingItems.name: Airflow measuring sensor (velocity pressure transducer)
missingItems.explanation: A pressure-independent VAV box requires an airflow sensor to measure actual airflow. Without it, the controller cannot regulate airflow to meet minimum ventilation or maximum cooling setpoints. Required by ASHRAE 62.1 and Guideline 36.

missingItems.name: Discharge air temperature sensor
missingItems.explanation: A discharge air temperature sensor after the reheat coil is needed to prevent overheating and to confirm the reheat valve is functioning correctly. Required by ASHRAE Guideline 36 for VAV boxes with reheat.

explanation: VAV box controls require airflow measurement for ventilation compliance per ASHRAE 62.1 and discharge air sensing for reheat control per Guideline 36. These sensors are essential for pressure-independent operation.

title: AHU Controls Package
description: A commercial air handling unit has controls installed. Check for missing control components.
presentItems: DDC controller in panel on AHU
Supply air temperature sensor in discharge duct
Supply fan VFD with BAS communication
Outdoor air damper with actuator
Chilled water valve with actuator
Hot water valve with actuator

missingItems.name: Mixed air temperature sensor
missingItems.explanation: A mixed air temperature sensor downstream of the mixing plenum is critical for economizer control and freeze protection. Without it, the controller cannot verify proper air mixing or detect freezing conditions. Required by ASHRAE Guideline 36.

missingItems.name: Duct static pressure sensor in supply duct
missingItems.explanation: A duct static pressure sensor (typically at 2/3 of the way down the longest duct run) is required for fan speed control. Without it, the VFD cannot modulate fan speed to maintain proper duct pressure. Required by ASHRAE 90.1 and Guideline 36.

missingItems.name: Filter differential pressure sensor or switch
missingItems.explanation: A differential pressure sensor across the filter bank monitors filter loading. Without it, there is no indication when filters need replacement, leading to excessive energy use from high pressure drop or poor air quality from bypassed filters.

explanation: Complete AHU controls require mixed air sensing for economizer/freeze protection, duct static pressure for fan control, and filter monitoring for maintenance. These three sensors are mandatory per ASHRAE Guideline 36.

title: Chiller Plant Controls
description: A chiller plant with two water-cooled chillers has controls installed. Identify missing control components.
presentItems: Plant DDC controller with chiller staging logic
Chilled water supply and return temperature sensors
Condenser water supply and return temperature sensors
Chiller enable/disable commands via BACnet
Primary chilled water pump status feedback
Cooling tower fan VFDs with BAS control

missingItems.name: Chilled water flow meter or differential pressure sensor
missingItems.explanation: A flow meter or DP sensor across the evaporator is needed to verify water flow before enabling the chiller and to calculate plant load (BTU metering). Running a chiller without confirmed flow causes evaporator freeze-up. Required by ASHRAE Guideline 36.

missingItems.name: Condenser water flow switch or meter
missingItems.explanation: Condenser water flow must be verified before chiller startup. Without a flow switch, the chiller could start with no condenser flow, causing high head pressure, compressor damage, and safety lockout. Required by chiller manufacturer sequences.

explanation: Chiller plant controls must verify water flow on both the evaporator and condenser sides before enabling chillers. Flow verification prevents catastrophic equipment damage and is required by both ASHRAE Guideline 36 and chiller manufacturers.

title: Boiler Plant Controls
description: A hot water boiler plant with two condensing boilers has controls installed. Check for missing components.
presentItems: Boiler plant DDC controller
Hot water supply and return temperature sensors
Boiler enable/disable via BACnet gateway
Hot water pump VFDs with BAS speed control
Outdoor air temperature sensor for reset schedule

missingItems.name: Hot water differential pressure sensor across building loop
missingItems.explanation: A differential pressure sensor across the supply and return mains is essential for variable-speed pump control. Without it, the pump VFDs cannot modulate speed to maintain system pressure as zone valves open and close. Required by ASHRAE 90.1 for variable-flow systems.

missingItems.name: Low water temperature safety interlock
missingItems.explanation: A low water temperature safety sensor is needed to prevent thermal shock to condensing boilers during cold starts and to interlock pump operation. Boilers require minimum return water temperature per manufacturer requirements to prevent flue gas condensation damage in non-condensing units.

explanation: Boiler plant controls require differential pressure sensing for variable-speed pump control per ASHRAE 90.1 and temperature safety interlocks to protect boiler equipment. These are frequently missing in controls submittals.

title: Lighting Control System
description: A lighting control system has been installed in an office building. Identify missing control components.
presentItems: Lighting control panel with relay modules
Occupancy sensors in private offices
Wall switches with override timers in open areas
BAS interface for scheduling
Daylight harvesting sensors at perimeter zones

missingItems.name: Photocell sensor for exterior lighting control
missingItems.explanation: Exterior lighting requires a photocell or astronomical time clock to turn on at dusk and off at dawn. Without it, exterior lighting runs on schedule only, wasting energy if the schedule doesn't match actual daylight. Required by ASHRAE 90.1 Section 9.4.1.

missingItems.name: Emergency lighting bypass or transfer relay
missingItems.explanation: Life-safety and egress lighting must remain on during emergencies regardless of automation commands. A bypass relay ensures emergency circuits are not controlled by the BAS or lighting control panel. Required by NEC Article 700 and IBC.

explanation: Lighting controls must include photocell sensing for exterior efficiency per ASHRAE 90.1 and emergency lighting bypass for life safety per NEC 700. Automated lighting must never override life-safety circuits.

title: BAS Network Infrastructure
description: A building automation network has been installed connecting multiple floors. Identify what's missing from the network infrastructure.
presentItems: BACnet/IP backbone on building Ethernet LAN
BACnet routers at each floor connecting MS/TP trunks to IP
Managed network switch for BAS traffic
BAS server connected to backbone
Field controllers daisy-chained on MS/TP trunks

missingItems.name: End-of-line termination resistors on each MS/TP trunk
missingItems.explanation: Each BACnet MS/TP trunk requires 120-ohm termination resistors at both physical ends of the bus to prevent signal reflections. Without termination, communication errors increase significantly, especially on longer trunk runs. Required by ASHRAE 135.

missingItems.name: Virtual Local Area Network (VLAN) segregation for BAS traffic
missingItems.explanation: BAS network traffic should be segregated from general IT traffic using a dedicated VLAN. Without VLAN separation, broadcast storms, network congestion, or security breaches on the IT network can disrupt building automation. Recommended by NIST SP 800-82 for industrial control systems.

distractors: Wireless access points for mobile BAS access
explanation: BAS networks require proper physical-layer termination (end-of-line resistors) per ASHRAE 135 and logical network segregation (VLAN) per NIST cybersecurity guidelines. Both are essential for reliable and secure BAS communication.

title: Energy Metering Installation
description: An energy metering system has been installed for a commercial building. Check for missing components.
presentItems: Main electrical meter with kWh pulse output
Current transformers on main electrical feeds
BAS controller receiving meter pulse data
Natural gas meter with pulse output to BAS

missingItems.name: BTU meter on chilled water plant (flow + temperature sensors)
missingItems.explanation: A BTU meter measuring chilled water flow and delta-T is essential for tracking cooling energy production and calculating chiller plant efficiency (kW/ton). Without it, there is no way to verify plant performance. Required by ASHRAE 90.1 for buildings over 25,000 sq ft.

missingItems.name: Electrical submeter on HVAC equipment panels
missingItems.explanation: Submetering HVAC electrical consumption separately from lighting and plug loads enables energy analysis and identifies waste. ASHRAE 90.1 Section 8 and many energy codes require tenant-level or system-level submetering for energy management.

explanation: Comprehensive energy metering requires both thermal (BTU) and electrical submetering to enable energy analysis and efficiency verification. ASHRAE 90.1 requires metering infrastructure for building performance monitoring.

title: Fire and Smoke System Integration
description: A BAS integration with the fire alarm system has been set up. Identify missing components.
presentItems: Fire alarm panel with BACnet gateway to BAS
Duct smoke detectors at AHUs reporting to fire alarm panel
BAS graphic showing fire alarm status points
AHU shutdown command on fire alarm activation
Smoke damper position monitoring on BAS

missingItems.name: Hardwired AHU shutdown independent of BAS
missingItems.explanation: Duct smoke detector shutdown of AHU fans must be hardwired directly to the fan starter or disconnect, independent of any BAS command. BAS-only shutdown is not acceptable for life-safety functions because software can fail. Required by NFPA 90A and NEC 725.3.

missingItems.name: Stairwell pressurization fan automatic start interlock
missingItems.explanation: Stairwell pressurization fans must start automatically on fire alarm activation through a hardwired connection from the fire alarm panel, not through a BAS software command. This is a life-safety function that must operate independently. Required by NFPA 92 and IBC 909.

explanation: Fire and smoke integration requires hardwired safety interlocks that operate independently of the BAS. The BAS can monitor and display status, but life-safety control functions must never depend solely on software per NFPA 90A and NFPA 92.

title: Building Pressurization Controls
description: Controls for maintaining positive building pressure in a commercial office have been installed. Check for missing components.
presentItems: Building static pressure sensor (indoor vs. outdoor)
Return fan VFD with BAS speed control
Supply fan VFD with BAS speed control
BAS control sequence for pressure offset

missingItems.name: Outdoor air measuring station at AHU intake
missingItems.explanation: An airflow measuring station at the outdoor air intake is needed to verify and control the actual amount of outdoor air being introduced. Without it, the system cannot confirm ASHRAE 62.1 ventilation minimums are met while maintaining building pressure.

missingItems.name: Relief or exhaust air path with motorized damper
missingItems.explanation: When more outdoor air is brought in than can be returned, excess air must have a relief path (barometric or motorized relief damper). Without it, the building over-pressurizes, causing doors to not close properly and excessive infiltration/exfiltration. Required by ASHRAE Guideline 36.

explanation: Building pressurization controls require accurate outdoor airflow measurement for ventilation verification and a properly controlled relief air path to prevent over-pressurization. Both are part of ASHRAE Guideline 36 multi-zone AHU sequences.

title: Laboratory Controls System
description: A laboratory HVAC and controls system has been installed for a research facility. Identify missing control components.
presentItems: Lab exhaust fan with VFD
Fume hood face velocity controllers
Room pressure monitor and controller
Supply air valve with flow controller
Temperature control with reheat for each lab
BAS integration for monitoring and alarms

missingItems.name: Room pressure offset tracking between supply and exhaust
missingItems.explanation: Labs require precise negative pressure relative to corridors by maintaining a fixed CFM offset between supply and exhaust airflow. The exhaust must always exceed supply by a specific amount (typically 50-200 CFM) to maintain containment. Required by ASHRAE 110 and ANSI Z9.5.

missingItems.name: Emergency exhaust mode with manual override
missingItems.explanation: A manual emergency purge button at the lab entrance must command maximum exhaust and minimum supply to purge hazardous fumes during a spill. This safety function must override normal BAS operation. Required by ANSI Z9.5 and NFPA 45.

missingItems.name: Exhaust air velocity or flow verification at each fume hood
missingItems.explanation: Each fume hood requires independent verification that face velocity meets the minimum (typically 80-100 FPM) with an alarm if velocity drops below the safety threshold. Without it, hazardous fumes can escape into the lab. Required by OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1450 and ASHRAE 110.

explanation: Laboratory controls are among the most complex and safety-critical in building automation. Pressure offset tracking, emergency purge capability, and fume hood velocity verification are all life-safety requirements per ASHRAE 110, ANSI Z9.5, and OSHA standards.

title: Clean Room Controls System
description: An ISO Class 7 clean room controls system has been installed in a pharmaceutical facility. Identify missing control components.
presentItems: Dedicated clean room AHU controller
Room temperature and humidity sensors
HEPA filter differential pressure monitoring
Supply fan VFD with BAS control
Room pressure displayed on local gauge
Airlock door interlock system

missingItems.name: Particle counter with continuous monitoring and alarm
missingItems.explanation: ISO 14644-1 Class 7 clean rooms require continuous particle monitoring to verify classification is maintained during operations. A particle counter with BAS alarm ensures immediate notification if contamination levels exceed limits. Required by ISO 14644-2.

missingItems.name: Cascade pressure control across all adjacent rooms
missingItems.explanation: Clean rooms must maintain a pressure cascade — each room at a specific pressure relative to adjacent spaces (typically 0.03-0.05 in. W.C. per boundary). The controls must maintain this cascade through all door openings and varying exhaust loads. Required by ISO 14644-4.

explanation: Clean room controls require continuous particle monitoring per ISO 14644-2 and cascade pressure control across room boundaries per ISO 14644-4. These are fundamental requirements for maintaining pharmaceutical-grade clean room classification.

title: CO2-Based Demand Ventilation System
description: A CO2-based demand controlled ventilation system has been installed in a conference room zone. Review the installation for missing components.
presentItems: Wall-mounted CO2 sensor in conference room
DDC controller programmed for DCV sequence
VAV box with modulating outdoor air damper
BACnet communication to BAS head-end
CO2 setpoint configured at 1000 ppm

missingItems.name: CO2 sensor calibration gas port
missingItems.explanation: University specifications require CO2 sensors with an accessible calibration gas port for field verification. CO2 sensors drift over time and must be calibrated against a known reference gas concentration. Without a calibration port, the sensor cannot be properly field-verified, leading to inaccurate ventilation control and potential indoor air quality problems.

missingItems.name: Outdoor air CO2 reference sensor
missingItems.explanation: Demand controlled ventilation systems should include an outdoor CO2 reference sensor to establish the baseline concentration. Outdoor CO2 varies by location (typically 400-450 ppm but can be higher near traffic). Without a reference, the system assumes a fixed baseline that may not reflect actual conditions, leading to under- or over-ventilation per ASHRAE 62.1.

explanation: CO2 demand ventilation requires proper sensor calibration capability and an outdoor reference measurement. University specs mandate calibration ports on all CO2 sensors because sensor drift is a known issue that directly affects indoor air quality and ASHRAE 62.1 ventilation compliance.

title: BACnet Controller Network Installation
description: A new BACnet/IP controller has been installed on the building automation network. Check for missing components per University specifications.
presentItems: BACnet/IP controller with web interface
Cat6A Ethernet cable to network switch
Controller programmed with sequences of operation
All I/O points wired and tested
Controller registered on BAS head-end

missingItems.name: BACnet communication card (B-OWS or B-AAC profile)
missingItems.explanation: University specifications require controllers to have a BACnet communication card supporting the appropriate device profile (B-OWS for operator workstations, B-AAC for advanced application controllers). The communication card ensures full BACnet interoperability including read/write property services, COV subscriptions, and alarm/event notification per ASHRAE 135-2016.

missingItems.name: Network time synchronization configuration
missingItems.explanation: BACnet controllers must be configured for network time synchronization (TimeSynchronization service) to ensure all trend logs, alarms, and events across the BAS have consistent timestamps. Without time sync, troubleshooting becomes extremely difficult when events on different controllers show different times. Required by ASHRAE 135 and University BAS specifications.

explanation: BACnet interoperability requires proper communication profiles and time synchronization across all controllers. University specs mandate ASHRAE 135-2016 compliance including standardized device profiles and consistent timestamps for effective building operations.

title: CRAC Unit Controls Installation
description: A Computer Room Air Conditioning (CRAC) unit has been installed on Level 32 of the high-rise commercial TI project with its iCOM controller. Review the installation for missing components.
presentItems: Liebert CRAC unit CRAC-32-1 installed and piped
iCOM controller mounted on CRAC unit for monitoring
Liebert vNSA network switch for lead/lag control of CRAC-32-1 and CRAC-32-2
Supply and return air temperature sensors connected
Refrigerant pressure gauges installed

missingItems.name: Leak detector connected to CRAC drip pan
missingItems.explanation: The high-rise office specification requires leak detectors at each CRAC unit connected to the drip pans. CRAC units handle refrigerant and condensate — a leak can cause water damage to the server/IDF room equipment below. The leak detector must alarm through the iCOM controller and the base building BMS for immediate response.

missingItems.name: BMS integration for remote monitoring and alarms
missingItems.explanation: The CRAC iCOM controller must be integrated with the base building BMS via BACnet for centralized monitoring. Without BMS integration, CRAC alarms (high temperature, leak, compressor failure) are only visible locally at the unit. Building engineers need remote visibility from the central workstation, especially for after-hours emergency response.

explanation: CRAC units in high-rise commercial TI spaces require leak detection at drip pans and BMS integration for remote monitoring. The iCOM controller manages local operation, but without leak detectors and BMS connectivity, critical alarms go unnoticed and water damage risks are unmitigated.

title: Exhaust Fan Timeclock Control
description: Exhaust fans in the high-rise commercial commercial office TI have been installed with timeclock control. Identify missing control components.
presentItems: Exhaust fans installed per mechanical plans
7-day programmable timeclock for scheduling
Fan motor wired through timeclock relay contact
Fan status indicator light at control panel

missingItems.name: Manual override switch with automatic return to schedule
missingItems.explanation: Timeclock-controlled exhaust fans must have a manual override switch for after-hours or emergency use. The override must automatically return to the timeclock schedule after a preset period (typically 2 hours) to prevent the fan from being left on indefinitely. Without an override, occupants during unscheduled hours have no ventilation. Required by ASHRAE 62.1 for spaces that may be occupied outside scheduled hours.

missingItems.name: Current sensing relay for fan status feedback to BMS
missingItems.explanation: A current sensing relay on the exhaust fan motor provides actual run status feedback to the BMS. Without it, the BMS only knows the timeclock schedule — not whether the fan is actually running. A failed motor or tripped breaker would go undetected until occupants complain of poor air quality. Required for LEED commissioning verification.

explanation: Exhaust fan timeclock control requires both a manual override for after-hours use and status feedback to the BMS. The override ensures ventilation is available when needed, while the current sensing relay confirms the fan is actually operating per ASHRAE 62.1 and LEED commissioning requirements.

title: BTU Metering Installation for Base Building BMS
description: BTU meters have been installed on the high-rise commercial TI project per base building standard. Review the installation for missing components.
presentItems: Ultrasonic flow meter installed on chilled water supply pipe
Supply water temperature sensor installed
Return water temperature sensor installed
BTU meter display showing real-time readings
BACnet communication module in BTU meter

missingItems.name: BTU meter data point mapping to base building BMS
missingItems.explanation: The high-rise office specification requires BTU meters to be tied to the base building BMS for usage monitoring. The BACnet module is installed but the points (total BTU, flow rate, delta-T, supply/return temperatures) must be mapped and trended in the BMS. Without this integration, the building management team cannot track tenant energy usage or allocate costs per the base building standard.

missingItems.name: BTU meter calibration verification certificate
missingItems.explanation: BTU meters must have a calibration verification per the base building standard. The flow meter accuracy and temperature sensor offsets must be documented and verified against reference instruments during commissioning. Without calibration records, the accuracy of energy billing data is unverifiable and may not satisfy LEED metering requirements.

distractors: Pressure gauge on chilled water pipe
explanation: The high-rise commercial office requires BTU meters integrated with the base building BMS for tenant energy usage monitoring. Both data point mapping and calibration verification are essential — the meter hardware alone does not satisfy the specification without BMS integration and documented accuracy per ASHRAE 90.1 Section 8.

title: California Title 24 Lighting Controls
description: Lighting controls for the high-rise commercial office TI project have been installed per California Title 24 requirements. Identify missing control components.
presentItems: Occupancy sensors installed in private offices and restrooms
Daylight-responsive controls at perimeter zones within 15 feet of windows
Manual on/off switches at each lighting control zone
Automatic shut-off via timeclock after business hours
Motorized shades (MS) on exterior windows per electrical plans

missingItems.name: Demand response signal acceptance capability
missingItems.explanation: California Title 24 requires lighting control systems to accept demand response signals for automated load shedding during peak grid events. The system must be capable of reducing lighting power by at least 15% in response to an external signal from the utility or building operator. Without this capability, the project fails Title 24 Section 130.1(d) requirements.

missingItems.name: Multi-level lighting control (minimum 2 switching levels or continuous dimming)
missingItems.explanation: Title 24 requires multi-level lighting control in spaces larger than 100 sq ft. Each lighting zone must have at least two switching levels (1/3 and 2/3 lamps) or continuous dimming capability. A single on/off switch does not satisfy the requirement for stepped or continuous light level adjustment per Title 24 Section 130.1(b).

explanation: California Title 24 lighting controls for commercial office spaces go beyond basic occupancy sensing and scheduling. Demand response capability and multi-level switching are mandatory requirements that are frequently overlooked during TI projects. The motorized shades complement the daylight controls but do not replace the need for demand response and multi-level switching.

title: High-Rise BAS Integration with Base Building Systems
description: A tenant improvement BAS has been installed on Levels 30-32 of the high-rise commercial at downtown office tower. The TI BAS must integrate with the base building systems. Identify missing integration components.
presentItems: TI DDC controllers installed for VAV systems on Levels 30-32
iCOM controllers on CRAC units with vNSA lead/lag switching
Motorized outside air dampers interlocked with evaporator fans
Firestat and smoke detectors installed in ductwork
Transfer fans with wall-mounted thermostat control at 80 degrees F

missingItems.name: BACnet gateway between TI BAS and base building BMS
missingItems.explanation: The TI controls must communicate with the base building BMS through a BACnet gateway or IP integration point. Without this gateway, the base building operations team cannot monitor TI HVAC systems, receive alarms, or coordinate energy management between tenant and base building systems. The high-rise office spec requires all TI systems to be visible on the base building BMS.

missingItems.name: Base building chilled water valve interlock with TI systems
missingItems.explanation: The TI VAV and CRAC systems use chilled water supplied by the base building plant. A control interlock must coordinate TI chilled water demand with the base building chiller staging. Without this interlock, the base building chillers may not stage properly for TI loads, and the BTU meters cannot accurately track tenant usage for cost allocation.

missingItems.name: Elevator recall and fire alarm integration for Levels 30-32
missingItems.explanation: The TI smoke detection and fire alarm systems must integrate with the base building fire alarm panel for elevator recall and stairwell pressurization on Levels 30-32. In a high-rise, fire events on tenant floors must trigger building-wide emergency responses through the base building systems per NFPA 72 and IBC Section 907.

distractors: Separate fire command center for tenant
explanation: High-rise TI projects at the high-rise office require comprehensive integration between tenant BAS and base building systems. BACnet gateway communication, chilled water coordination, and fire alarm integration are essential for safe operation and facilities management in a multi-tenant high-rise per ASHRAE 135, NFPA 72, and IBC requirements.
